text: The laparoscopic view shows the surgeon using a lateral approach for distal pancreatectomy, with visible port placements in the abdomen. The surgical field is exposed, revealing the pancreas and tumor being mobilized upwards, while the vessels remain in the background before the pancreas is stapled.

The surgeon mobilizes the colon and splenic flexure, revealing the inferior edge of the pancreas. The lesser sac is entered near the hilus of the spleen to clear the view of dangling fat. The surgical field is fully exposed, showing the anterior surface of the pancreas with a visible cystic neoplasm.

The surgeon mobilizes and identifies the inferior edge of the pancreas, revealing the splenic vein posteriorly. The superior surface of the pancreas is visible, with the splenic artery being dissected out. The dissection progresses towards the tail of the pancreas, where the splenic vein is carefully separated from the pancreas parenchyma along the superior edge. The surgeon prepares to flip up the tip of the tail of the pancreas to gradually separate it from the surrounding vessels.

The surgeon uses ultrasonic shears to sever small branches off the splenic vein, creating a window to lift the tip of the tail of the pancreas away from the splenic artery and vein. The attachments connecting the splenic artery to the pancreas tail are also cut with ultrasonic shears, as the pancreas is mobilized from lateral to medial using cautery and shears.

The surgeon uses a blue load endoscopic stapler to transect the pancreas, creating a clean staple line. The pancreas is visibly mobile, and the cystic lesion is proximal to the staple line. The surrounding vessels, including the artery and vein, remain intact, and the spleen is well perfused, though there is slight ischemia at the inferior pole.
